questions.question_text: Minnesota uses a dual recording system. What are the two systems?
distractors: Grantor-grantee and tract index
County recorder and state registrar
Race-notice and pure notice
questions.explanation: Minnesota maintains two parallel recording systems: the abstract (regular) system recorded with the county recorder, and the Torrens system administered by the county registrar of titles.

questions.question_text: Two buyers each claim ownership of the same parcel recorded in the abstract system. Buyer A recorded first. Who has priority?
questions.correct_answer: Buyer A, based on first in time recording
distractors: Buyer B, if they paid a higher purchase price
Neither, until a court determines ownership
Buyer B, if they took possession first
questions.explanation: In the abstract recording system, priority is determined by 'first in time, first in right.' The party who records first has superior claim, providing constructive notice to subsequent purchasers.

questions.question_text: What type of notice does recording a deed in the abstract system provide?
distractors: Actual notice only
Implied notice
No notice until title search
questions.explanation: Recording a document in the abstract system provides constructive notice to the world. Anyone could have discovered the recorded interest through a diligent title search.

questions.question_text: A lender records a mortgage on Monday at 10 a.m. Another lender records a mortgage on the same property Monday at 2 p.m. Which has priority?
distractors: Both have equal priority as same-day recordings
The larger mortgage amount takes priority
Priority depends on when funds were disbursed
questions.explanation: Under the abstract system, priority is determined by date and time of recording. The mortgage recorded at 10 a.m. was first in time and therefore has superior priority.

questions.question_text: An agent explains recording to a client. Which statement about Minnesota's abstract system is most accurate?
questions.correct_answer: Documents are filed with the county recorder
distractors: All land in the county must use this system
Recording guarantees the title is free of defects
The county issues a certificate of title at recording
questions.explanation: In the abstract system, documents are recorded with the county recorder. Not all land must use this system (some is Torrens), recording does not guarantee title quality, and certificates of title are a Torrens feature.

questions.question_text: Under the Torrens system, what document serves as conclusive evidence of ownership?
distractors: Recorded warranty deed
Abstract of title
Title insurance policy
questions.explanation: In the Torrens system under Ch. 508, the Certificate of Title issued by the county registrar is conclusive evidence of ownership. This differs from the abstract system where a chain of recorded deeds must be examined.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases Torrens property and receives a deed but does not file for a new certificate. What is the effect?
questions.correct_answer: The transfer is incomplete until a new certificate is filed
distractors: The deed alone transfers full legal title to the buyer
The county automatically issues a new certificate of title
The buyer owns the property but cannot sell or encumber it
questions.explanation: In the Torrens system, merely recording a deed is insufficient. A new Certificate of Title must be filed with the county registrar to complete the transfer and reflect the new owner.

questions.question_text: A clerical error in the Torrens system causes a property owner to lose title. What fund provides compensation?
distractors: County general reserve fund
State real estate recovery fund
Title insurance guaranty fund
questions.explanation: The Torrens system maintains an assurance fund to compensate parties who suffer losses due to errors in the system. This fund provides a financial remedy when the certificate of title contains mistakes.

questions.question_text: Why is Minnesota notable among U.S. states regarding the Torrens system?
questions.correct_answer: It is one of few states still actively using it
distractors: It invented the Torrens system in 1858
All Minnesota land must be registered under Torrens
It is the only state with a Torrens assurance fund
questions.explanation: Minnesota is one of only a few states that still actively uses the Torrens title registration system. Not all Minnesota land is Torrens-registered; property owners may choose to bring land into the system.

questions.question_text: A title examiner discovers that a parcel is registered under both the abstract and Torrens systems. Which analysis is correct?
questions.correct_answer: Land is registered under one system, not both simultaneously
distractors: The abstract registration automatically overrides Torrens
Both registrations are valid and provide dual protection
The owner must pay fees under both systems annually
questions.explanation: A parcel is registered under either the abstract or Torrens system, not both. If this situation appears, it indicates an error that must be resolved. Land can be brought into Torrens from the abstract system through a court proceeding.

questions.question_text: A home sells for $350,000 and the buyer assumes a $100,000 mortgage. What is the deed tax owed?
distractors: $1,155 based on the full sale price
$350 based on the assumed mortgage
$500 as a flat fee per transaction
questions.explanation: Minnesota deed tax is 0.33% of net consideration (sale price minus assumed mortgages). Net consideration is $350,000 - $100,000 = $250,000. The tax is $250,000 x 0.0033 = $825.

questions.question_text: Who is primarily responsible for paying the deed tax in Minnesota?
distractors: The buyer
Both parties equally
The title company
questions.explanation: In Minnesota, the deed tax is paid by the seller. The rate is 0.33% of the net consideration, which is the sale price minus any assumed mortgages.

questions.question_text: A borrower takes out a $400,000 conventional mortgage in Hennepin County. What mortgage registry tax is due?
distractors: $920 at 0.23% only
$1,320 at 0.33% of the loan
$0 because residential loans are exempt
questions.explanation: The mortgage registry tax is 0.23% of the debt secured. In Hennepin County, an additional 0.01% ERT applies, making the combined rate 0.24%. $400,000 x 0.0024 = $960.

questions.question_text: Which type of loan is typically exempt from Minnesota's mortgage registry tax?
distractors: Conventional loans under $500,000
Commercial loans from state-chartered banks
Second mortgages on residential property
questions.explanation: Minnesota exempts government-backed loans (such as FHA and VA) from the mortgage registry tax. Conventional loans are not exempt regardless of size.

questions.question_text: A property in Ramsey County sells for $500,000 with no assumed mortgage. The buyer also records a $400,000 mortgage. Which taxes include the Environmental Response Fund Tax surcharge?
questions.correct_answer: Both deed tax and mortgage registry tax
distractors: Only the deed tax on the sale
Only the mortgage registry tax on the loan
Neither tax applies in Ramsey County
questions.explanation: Hennepin and Ramsey counties add a 0.01% Environmental Response Fund Tax (ERT) surcharge to both the deed tax and the mortgage registry tax. This is unique to these two counties.
